title: Death rate by cause
subtitle: The estimated annual death rate per 100,000 people from broad categories of causes of death. Injuries (such as accidents, violence and suicides); communicable, maternal, neonatal and nutritional diseases; and [non-communicable diseases](#dod:non-communicable-diseases).
caption:
Death rate by cause, World

The estimated annual death rate per 100,000 people from broad categories of causes of death. Injuries (such as accidents, violence and suicides); communicable, maternal, neonatal and nutritional diseases; and non-communicable diseases.

This stacked area chart tracks the global age-standardized death rate per 100,000 people from 1980 to 2023, divided into three cause categories: non-communicable diseases (NCDs) in pink at the base, communicable, maternal, neonatal and nutritional diseases in green in the middle layer, and injuries in tan at the top. The total death rate begins near 1,300 per 100,000 in 1980 and declines steadily to approximately 700 by 2023, with a brief upward spike around 2020 before resuming its downward trajectory. Non-communicable diseases start near 800 per 100,000 in 1980, dip slightly through the 1990s, then stabilize around 500–550 from 2010 onward, forming the largest single component throughout the period. Communicable, maternal, neonatal and nutritional diseases begin near 400 per 100,000 in 1980 and fall consistently to under 200 by 2023, showing the steepest proportional decline of the three categories. Injuries remain the smallest layer, hovering between 100 and 150 per 100,000 across the entire timeframe, with a slight dip around 2010 and a small rise after 2020. The metric is age-standardized to allow comparisons between countries and over time. The most visible pattern is the sustained reduction in deaths from communicable, maternal, neonatal and nutritional diseases, while non-communicable diseases persist as the dominant cause of death globally despite overall mortality falling.
